title: DSA Fundamentals
description: Core data structures, algorithms, and interview patterns.

cards.back: A way to describe the upper bound growth rate of an algorithm as input size increases.
Constant time complexity. Runtime does not depend on input size.
Logarithmic complexity. Input size is repeatedly reduced, such as in binary search.
Linear complexity. Runtime grows proportionally with input size.
Typical complexity for efficient sorting algorithms such as Merge Sort and Heap Sort.
Quadratic complexity. Common in nested-loop algorithms.

cards.front: When should a hash map be considered?
cards.back: When fast O(1) average lookup, insertion, or frequency counting is needed.
When multiple keys map to the same hash bucket.

cards.front: What is the primary advantage of a hash set?
cards.back: Fast membership testing.

cards.front: When is the Two Pointers pattern useful?
cards.back: When working with sorted arrays, pairs, windows, or opposite-end traversal.

cards.front: What is the Sliding Window pattern?
cards.back: Maintaining a dynamic range of elements while expanding and shrinking boundaries.

cards.front: When is Sliding Window preferable to nested loops?
cards.back: When processing contiguous subarrays or substrings efficiently.

cards.front: What problem type is Binary Search designed for?
cards.back: Searching within a sorted search space.

cards.front: What condition must hold for Binary Search?
cards.back: The search space must be monotonic or sorted.

cards.front: What is the time complexity of Binary Search?
cards.back: O(log n).
A sequence of nodes where each node points to the next node.
Efficient insertion and deletion when node references are known.

cards.front: What is the main disadvantage of linked lists?
cards.back: No O(1) random access.

cards.front: What is Floyd's Cycle Detection algorithm?
cards.back: A slow and fast pointer technique used to detect cycles in linked lists.
A Last-In-First-Out data structure.

cards.front: What operations are performed on a stack?
cards.back: Push, pop, peek, and isEmpty.
When finding next greater, next smaller, or maintaining ordered elements.
A First-In-First-Out data structure.
A double-ended queue supporting insertion and removal from both ends.
A hierarchical data structure consisting of nodes connected by edges.
The topmost node of a tree.
A node with no children.
The number of edges from the root to a node.
The longest path from a node to a leaf.
A tree where each node has at most two children.

cards.front: What is a Binary Search Tree?
cards.back: A binary tree where left values are smaller and right values are larger.
Left subtree, node, right subtree.
Node, left subtree, right subtree.
Left subtree, right subtree, node.
Depth-First Search. Explore as far as possible before backtracking.
Breadth-First Search. Explore level by level.

cards.front: When is BFS preferred over DFS?
cards.back: When shortest path in an unweighted graph is required.
A complete binary tree that satisfies the heap property.
The smallest element is always at the root.
The largest element is always at the root.

cards.front: What is the primary use of heaps?
cards.back: Efficient access to minimum or maximum elements.
A queue where elements are processed based on priority.
A collection of vertices connected by edges.
A graph representation storing neighbors for each vertex.
A graph representation using a matrix of edge relationships.
A graph where every node is reachable from every other node.

cards.front: What is a cycle in a graph?
cards.back: A path that starts and ends at the same node.
A linear ordering of nodes in a directed acyclic graph.
A data structure for tracking connected components.

cards.front: What are the two key Union Find operations?
cards.back: Find and Union.
An optimization that flattens Union Find trees.
Solving problems by storing and reusing solutions to overlapping subproblems.

cards.front: What are the two requirements for Dynamic Programming?
cards.back: Overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure.
Top-down DP using caching.
Bottom-up DP using iterative computation.
Choosing items where each item can be taken at most once.

cards.front: What is the Longest Common Subsequence problem?
cards.back: Finding the longest sequence appearing in order within two strings.
Exploring solutions recursively and undoing choices when necessary.

cards.front: What types of problems commonly use Backtracking?
cards.back: Permutations, combinations, subsets, and constraint satisfaction.
A tree structure optimized for prefix-based string operations.
Autocomplete, dictionaries, prefix matching, and word search.
Making the locally optimal choice at each step.
When local optimal choices lead to a global optimum.
Selecting non-overlapping intervals using a greedy strategy.
An O(n) algorithm for finding the maximum subarray sum.
An algorithm for shortest paths in weighted graphs with nonnegative weights.

cards.front: What data structure is commonly used with Dijkstra?
cards.back: A min-heap priority queue.

cards.front: What is the difference between BFS and Dijkstra?
cards.back: BFS works for unweighted graphs, Dijkstra handles weighted graphs.

cards.front: What is the Fast and Slow Pointer pattern?
cards.back: Two pointers moving at different speeds to detect cycles or middle elements.

cards.front: What is the Merge Intervals pattern?
cards.back: Sort intervals and combine overlapping ranges.

cards.front: What is the Top K Elements pattern?
cards.back: Using heaps to efficiently track the k largest or smallest elements.

cards.front: What is the main goal of technical interview preparation?
cards.back: Recognizing patterns and applying the appropriate data structure or algorithm efficiently.
